questions.stem: For two interacting particles of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$, the reduced mass $\mu$ is defined as which of the following?
questions.explanation: The reduced mass is defined by $\mu = m_1 m_2/(m_1 + m_2)$, equivalently $1/\mu = 1/m_1 + 1/m_2$. It is the effective mass that appears when the two-body relative motion is rewritten as a single equivalent one-body problem. The sum $m_1 + m_2$ is the total mass, a different quantity that governs the centre-of-mass motion.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles have masses $m_1 = 2\ \text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 3\ \text{kg}$. What is the reduced mass of the system?
questions.explanation: Substituting into $\mu = m_1 m_2/(m_1 + m_2)$ gives $\mu = (2)(3)/(2+3) = 6/5 = 1.2\ \text{kg}$. The reduced mass is smaller than either individual mass, which is always the case. The trap is $5\ \text{kg}$, which is the total mass $m_1 + m_2$ rather than the reduced mass.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles each of mass $m$ interact with each other. What is the reduced mass of the pair?
questions.explanation: With $m_1 = m_2 = m$, the reduced mass is $\mu = m\cdot m/(m + m) = m^2/(2m) = m/2$. So for two equal masses the reduced mass is exactly half of one of them. The trap is $2m$, which adds the masses instead of forming the reduced mass.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A hydrogen-like pair has masses $m_1 = 4\ \text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 12\ \text{kg}$. What is the reduced mass?
questions.explanation: The reduced mass is $\mu = (4)(12)/(4 + 12) = 48/16 = 3\ \text{kg}$. It is less than the smaller mass $4\ \text{kg}$, as the reduced mass always is. The trap is $16\ \text{kg}$, the total mass, which confuses the denominator of the formula with its result.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: In the limit where $m_2$ is very much larger than $m_1$, what does the reduced mass $\mu = m_1 m_2/(m_1+m_2)$ approach?
questions.explanation: When $m_2 \gg m_1$, the denominator $m_1 + m_2 \approx m_2$, so $\mu \approx m_1 m_2/m_2 = m_1$. Physically, a very heavy partner barely moves, and the relative motion is governed almost entirely by the light particle's mass. This is why the reduced mass of an electron orbiting a heavy nucleus is essentially the electron mass.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: In the two-body problem, the relative coordinate is usually defined as which of the following?
questions.explanation: The relative coordinate is the displacement of particle 1 from particle 2, $\vec{r} = \vec{r}_1 - \vec{r}_2$, and it measures the separation that the interaction depends on. The mass-weighted average $\tfrac{1}{M}(m_1\vec{r}_1 + m_2\vec{r}_2)$ is instead the centre-of-mass coordinate. Together the relative and centre-of-mass coordinates replace the two original position vectors.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: When the two-body problem with internal force depending only on the separation is reduced to an equivalent one-body problem, the equation of motion for the relative coordinate is which of the following?
questions.explanation: Subtracting the two single-particle equations of motion and using the reduced mass gives $\mu\ddot{\vec{r}} = \vec{F}(\vec{r})$, the motion of a single fictitious particle of mass $\mu$ subject to the interaction force. This is the heart of the reduction: a two-body problem becomes one equivalent one-body problem. The total mass $M$ governs the separate, force-free motion of the centre of mass.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The two-body problem is conventionally split into two independent motions. Which pair correctly names them?
questions.explanation: By changing to centre-of-mass and relative coordinates, the two-body problem separates into the free motion of the centre of mass, governed by the total mass, and the relative motion, governed by the reduced mass and the interaction. The two motions decouple completely when the internal force depends only on the separation. This separation is what makes the reduced-mass picture so useful.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two stars have masses $m_1 = 3M_\odot$ and $m_2 = 6M_\odot$, where $M_\odot$ is one solar mass. What is the reduced mass of the binary, in units of $M_\odot$?
questions.explanation: The reduced mass is $\mu = (3)(6)/(3 + 6) = 18/9 = 2M_\odot$. It is smaller than the lighter star, as required. The trap is $9M_\odot$, the total mass of the binary, which is the denominator of the formula rather than the reduced mass itself.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: In the centre-of-mass frame, the total kinetic energy of two interacting particles can be written compactly using the reduced mass. If $\vec{v} = \dot{\vec{r}}$ is the relative velocity, the kinetic energy of relative motion is which expression?
questions.explanation: Working in the centre-of-mass frame, the kinetic energy of the relative motion collapses to that of a single particle of mass $\mu$ moving with the relative velocity: $T_{\text{rel}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu v^2$. This mirrors the equation of motion $\mu\ddot{\vec{r}} = \vec{F}$, with the reduced mass playing the role of an ordinary mass. Using the total mass $M$ instead would double-count the inertia of the relative motion.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles with reduced mass $\mu = 1.2\ \text{kg}$ approach each other with a relative speed of $5\ \text{m/s}$. What is the kinetic energy associated with the relative motion?
questions.explanation: The kinetic energy of the relative motion is $T_{\text{rel}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu v^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(5)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(25) = 15\ \text{J}$. The relative speed must be squared before halving and multiplying by the reduced mass. The trap is $30\ \text{J}$, which forgets the factor of one half.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles of masses $m_1 = 2\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 0$ and $m_2 = 3\ \text{kg}$ at $x = 10\ \text{m}$ form a system. Taking the relative coordinate as $r = x_1 - x_2$, what is its value, and where is the centre of mass?
questions.explanation: The relative coordinate is $r = x_1 - x_2 = 0 - 10 = -10\ \text{m}$, the displacement of particle 1 from particle 2. The centre of mass is $X = (2\cdot 0 + 3\cdot 10)/(2 + 3) = 30/5 = 6\ \text{m}$, nearer the heavier mass. The two new coordinates fully replace $x_1$ and $x_2$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The original positions $\vec{r}_1$ and $\vec{r}_2$ can be recovered from the centre-of-mass position $\vec{R}$ and the relative coordinate $\vec{r} = \vec{r}_1 - \vec{r}_2$. Which formula gives $\vec{r}_1$?
questions.explanation: Solving $\vec{R} = (m_1\vec{r}_1 + m_2\vec{r}_2)/M$ together with $\vec{r} = \vec{r}_1 - \vec{r}_2$ gives $\vec{r}_1 = \vec{R} + (m_2/M)\vec{r}$ and $\vec{r}_2 = \vec{R} - (m_1/M)\vec{r}$. Each particle is displaced from the centre of mass by a share of the separation set by the other particle's mass. The trap uses $m_1$ in the numerator, which would place the heavier particle farther from the centre of mass, the opposite of the truth.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two masses $m$ and $2m$ are connected by a spring and oscillate along a line, free of external forces. The relative motion behaves as a single oscillator with what effective mass?
questions.explanation: The relative motion of a two-body system is governed by the reduced mass, $\mu = m\cdot 2m/(m + 2m) = 2m^2/(3m) = 2m/3$. So the spring drives an effective oscillator of mass $2m/3$, not of either individual mass. The trap is $3m$, the total mass, which controls the free drift of the centre of mass rather than the oscillation.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Which statement best describes why introducing the reduced mass is useful in the two-body problem?
options: It eliminates the interaction force entirely
It converts a two-body relative-motion problem into a mathematically equivalent one-body problem
It makes the total momentum zero in every frame
questions.explanation: The reduced mass lets the relative coordinate obey $\mu\ddot{\vec{r}} = \vec{F}(\vec{r})$, which is identical in form to the equation for a single particle of mass $\mu$ in an external field. So the whole machinery developed for one-body motion can be applied directly. The interaction force is not removed; it simply now acts on one fictitious particle instead of two real ones.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A binary system has masses $m_1 = 5\ \text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 20\ \text{kg}$. What is the reduced mass?
questions.explanation: The reduced mass is $\mu = (5)(20)/(5 + 20) = 100/25 = 4\ \text{kg}$. It lies below the lighter mass $5\ \text{kg}$, consistent with the heavy partner barely moving. The trap is $25\ \text{kg}$, the total mass, which is the denominator of the formula, not its value.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The total kinetic energy of a two-particle system can be written as $T = \tfrac{1}{2}M V^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\mu v^2$, where $V$ is the centre-of-mass speed and $v$ the relative speed. What do the two terms represent?
options: Translational energy of the centre of mass and energy of the relative motion
Energy of particle 1 and energy of particle 2 directly
Rotational energy and vibrational energy only
questions.explanation: The first term, $\tfrac{1}{2}MV^2$, is the kinetic energy of the whole mass moving with the centre of mass, and the second, $\tfrac{1}{2}\mu v^2$, is the kinetic energy of the relative motion in the centre-of-mass frame. This is exactly the reduced-mass form of Konig's theorem for two particles. The cross term vanishes because of the definitions of the centre-of-mass and relative coordinates.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles of masses $1\ \text{kg}$ and $1\ \text{kg}$ have a reduced mass that is used in a relative-motion energy calculation. If their relative speed is $4\ \text{m/s}$, what is the kinetic energy of relative motion?
questions.explanation: For two equal $1\ \text{kg}$ masses, $\mu = (1)(1)/(1+1) = 0.5\ \text{kg}$. The kinetic energy of relative motion is $\tfrac{1}{2}\mu v^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5)(4)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5)(16) = 4\ \text{J}$. The trap is $8\ \text{J}$, which uses the total mass $1\ \text{kg}$ for $\mu$ instead of the reduced mass $0.5\ \text{kg}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: An electron of mass $m_e$ orbits a proton of mass $m_p \approx 1836\,m_e$. Compared with treating the proton as infinitely heavy, using the reduced mass $\mu$ has what effect on $\mu$?
questions.explanation: The reduced mass is $\mu = m_e m_p/(m_e + m_p) = m_e/(1 + m_e/m_p)$, which is just below $m_e$ because the denominator slightly exceeds one. Treating the proton as infinitely heavy sets $\mu = m_e$ exactly; the finite proton mass lowers it by a small fraction of about $1/1837$. This tiny correction is measurable in atomic spectra.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Which equation correctly relates the reciprocal of the reduced mass to the individual masses?
questions.explanation: Starting from $\mu = m_1 m_2/(m_1 + m_2)$ and taking the reciprocal gives $1/\mu = (m_1 + m_2)/(m_1 m_2) = 1/m_1 + 1/m_2$. The reduced mass combines like resistors in parallel or capacitors in series. This reciprocal form is often the quickest way to compute $\mu$ by hand.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: In the centre-of-mass frame of a two-body system, what is the total linear momentum?
options: Equal to the momentum of the heavier particle
questions.explanation: The centre-of-mass frame is defined precisely as the frame in which the centre of mass is at rest, so the total momentum $\vec{P} = M\vec{V}$ is zero there. The two particles then carry equal and opposite momenta. This vanishing total momentum is what removes the cross term in the kinetic-energy split.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles have masses $m_1 = 2\ \text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 2\ \text{kg}$. What is the reduced mass?
questions.explanation: For equal masses the reduced mass is half of one of them: $\mu = (2)(2)/(2 + 2) = 4/4 = 1\ \text{kg}$. This matches the general rule $\mu = m/2$ for two masses each equal to $m$. The trap is $4\ \text{kg}$, the total mass, which is the denominator times two, not the reduced mass.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: The relative velocity of two particles is $\vec{v} = \dot{\vec{r}}_1 - \dot{\vec{r}}_2$. Particle 1 moves at $(3, 0)\ \text{m/s}$ and particle 2 at $(0, 4)\ \text{m/s}$. What is the magnitude of the relative velocity?
questions.explanation: The relative velocity is $\vec{v} = (3, 0) - (0, 4) = (3, -4)\ \text{m/s}$, and its magnitude is $\sqrt{3^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5\ \text{m/s}$. The components are subtracted first, then combined by Pythagoras. The trap is $7\ \text{m/s}$, which adds the two speeds as if the velocities were parallel.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles have masses $m_1 = 2\ \text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 3\ \text{kg}$. Find the total mass and the reduced mass.
questions.solution: The total mass is $M = m_1 + m_2 = $ {{1}} kg. The reduced mass is $\mu = m_1 m_2/M = 6/M = $ {{2}} kg. The reduced mass is always {{3}} than either individual mass.
questions.explanation: The total mass is $5\ \text{kg}$ and the reduced mass is $6/5 = 1.2\ \text{kg}$. Since $\mu = m_1 m_2/(m_1 + m_2)$ has a denominator larger than either factor, $\mu$ is always less than the smaller of the two masses.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: A binary star system has masses $m_1 = 4\ \text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 12\ \text{kg}$. Find the reduced mass.
questions.solution: The product is $m_1 m_2 = 4 \times 12 = 48$ and the sum is $m_1 + m_2 = $ {{1}} kg. The reduced mass is $\mu = 48/16 = $ {{2}} kg.
questions.explanation: The sum of the masses is $16\ \text{kg}$, so $\mu = 48/16 = 3\ \text{kg}$. This is less than the lighter mass of $4\ \text{kg}$, as the reduced mass always must be.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two equal masses, each $m = 6\ \text{kg}$, interact. Find their reduced mass using the general rule for equal masses.
questions.solution: For two equal masses the reduced mass is half of one of them, so $\mu = m/2 = 6/2 = $ {{1}} kg. Checking with the full formula, $\mu = (6)(6)/(6+6) = 36/12 = $ {{2}} kg, which agrees.
questions.explanation: The shortcut $\mu = m/2$ gives $3\ \text{kg}$, and the full formula $36/12 = 3\ \text{kg}$ confirms it. For equal masses the two approaches always coincide.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles with reduced mass $\mu = 1.2\ \text{kg}$ have a relative speed of $5\ \text{m/s}$. Find the kinetic energy of the relative motion.
questions.solution: The relative speed squared is $5^2 = 25$. The kinetic energy of relative motion is $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu v^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(25) = $ {{1}} joules. The mass that appears in this formula is the {{2}} mass.
questions.explanation: The relative kinetic energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(25) = 15\ \text{J}$. The relative motion behaves like a single particle of the reduced mass $\mu$, which is why $\mu$ rather than the total mass appears here.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Two particles of masses $m_1 = 2\ \text{kg}$ at $x_1 = 0$ and $m_2 = 3\ \text{kg}$ at $x_2 = 10\ \text{m}$ form a system. Find the relative coordinate $r = x_1 - x_2$ and the centre-of-mass position.
questions.solution: The relative coordinate is $r = x_1 - x_2 = 0 - 10 = $ {{1}} metres. The centre of mass is $X = (2\cdot 0 + 3\cdot 10)/5 = $ {{2}} metres, which lies nearer the {{3}} particle.
questions.explanation: The relative coordinate is $-10\ \text{m}$, the position of particle 1 measured from particle 2. The centre of mass is $30/5 = 6\ \text{m}$, closer to the heavier $3\ \text{kg}$ particle at $x = 10\ \text{m}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)

questions.stem: Describe the reduction of the two-body problem to an equivalent one-body problem.
questions.solution: The change to centre-of-mass and relative coordinates splits the motion into the free motion of the centre of mass, governed by the {{1}} mass, and the relative motion. The relative coordinate obeys $\mu\ddot{\vec{r}} = \vec{F}(\vec{r})$, the equation of a single particle of {{2}} mass. In the centre-of-mass frame the total momentum is {{3}} (give the number).
questions.explanation: The centre of mass moves freely with the total mass $M$, while the relative motion is that of a fictitious particle of reduced mass $\mu$ in the interaction field. The centre-of-mass frame is defined by zero total momentum, which is what makes the split clean.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Theoretical Mechanics II course (MATH 364)
